content.problemStatement:
For an array AAA of length NNN, we define f(A)f(A)f(A) to be the smallest non-negative integer KKK such that AAA can be sorted in ascending order by using the following operation several (possibly, zero) times:


Choose an index iii (1‚â§i<N1 \le i \lt N1‚â§i<N) such that Ai‚äïAi+1‚â§KA_i \oplus A_{i+1} \le KAi‚Äã‚äïAi+1‚Äã‚â§K.
Then, swap AiA_iAi‚Äã with Ai+1A_{i+1}Ai+1‚Äã.
Here, ‚äï\oplus‚äï denotes the bitwise XOR operation.

You're given two integers NNN and KKK.

Find any array AAA of length NNN satisfying the following properties:


1‚â§Ai‚â§N1 \le A_i \le N1‚â§Ai‚Äã‚â§N for each 1‚â§i‚â§N1 \le i \le N1‚â§i‚â§N, and
Ai‚âÝAjA_i \ne A_jAi‚ÄãÓÄÝ=Aj‚Äã for 1‚â§i<j‚â§N1 \le i \lt j \le N1‚â§i<j‚â§N, and
f(A)=Kf(A) = Kf(A)=K.
If there are multiple possible solutions, you may find any of them.

If no solution exists, print ‚àí1-1‚àí1.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single line of input, containing two space-separated integers NNN and KKK ‚Äî the length of the array AAA and the required value of f(A)f(A)f(A), respectively.


Output Format
For each test case,



If no solution exists, print the single integer ‚àí1-1‚àí1 on a new line.
Otherwise, output NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1‚Äã,A2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äã - the solution you found.

If there are multiple possible solutions, you may find any of them.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2‚ãÖ1052\cdot 10^52‚ãÖ105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: For A=[1,2,3,4]A = [1, 2, 3, 4]A=[1,2,3,4], we don't need any swaps. So, we have f(A)=0=Kf(A) = 0 = Kf(A)=0=K, as needed.
Test case 222: There are two possible arrays with N=2N = 2N=2: [1,2][1, 2][1,2] and [2,1][2, 1][2,1].

For A=[1,2]A = [1, 2]A=[1,2], we have f(A)=0f(A) = 0f(A)=0 since no swaps are needed.
For A=[2,1]A = [2, 1]A=[2,1], we're forced to swap 111 and 222 to sort it. Since 1‚äï2=31\oplus 2 = 31‚äï2=3, we need to choose some KKK that's ‚â•3\ge 3‚â•3. Of course, the smallest valid choice is hence K=3K = 3K=3; so we obtain f(A)=3f(A) = 3f(A)=3.
It can hence be seen that there's no length 222 array satisfying the conditions, so we print ‚àí1-1‚àí1.

Test case 333: For A=[3,2,5,1,4]A = [3, 2, 5, 1, 4]A=[3,2,5,1,4], it can be proved that AAA can be sorted in ascending order by choosing K=4K = 4K=4, whereas it cannot be sorted with K=3K = 3K=3.
This makes f(A)=4f(A) = 4f(A)=4, as required.
